filer: Distributed Capacity Parties (Cloverleaf Infrastructure, Sparkfund, Voltus)
overall_summary: The Distributed Capacity Parties, a group of demand-side and distributed resource companies, support the ANOPR's goal of faster large-load interconnection and urge FERC to direct RTOs/ISOs to count distributed capacity (load flexibility, storage, distributed generation, and grid enhancing technologies) toward that goal. Their core asks are to broaden ANOPR Principle Three so loads are studied together with distributed capacity in the relevant footprint, to broaden Principle Seven so expedited interconnection rewards loads that bring locationally relevant capacity even when it is not physically co-located, and to remove barriers to aggregated DER participation such as delays in Order No. 2222 implementation. They take no position on FERC's jurisdiction.

quotes.text: the Distributed Capacity Parties respectfully urge the Federal Energy Regulatory Commission (“FERC” or “Commission”) in any subsequent rulemaking2 to direct the Regional Transmission Organizations/Independent System Operators (“RTO/ISOs”) to develop mechanisms for granting expedited interconnection studies to large loads that provide curtailable and/or dispatchable distributed assets
quotes.concern: Urges FERC to direct RTOs/ISOs to expedite studies for large loads bringing curtailable or dispatchable distributed assets

quotes.text: The ANOPR Seventh Principle4 should include incremental Distributed Capacity in the form of load flexibility, storage, and/or generation capacity whose location can address capacity and transmission constraints projected to be caused by the new large loads, even if such capacity is located on the distribution system.
quotes.concern: Asks that Principle Seven count distributed capacity addressing constraints even when located on the distribution system

quotes.text: The Distributed Capacity Parties do not take a position on FERC’s jurisdiction.
quotes.concern: Takes no position on FERC's jurisdiction over large load interconnection

quotes.text: Distributed capacity assets are installed today across critical footprints, are readily installable en masse, and may be aggregated into utility-scale assets on a timeline of months rather than years.
quotes.concern: Argues distributed capacity can scale in months while new generation takes years

quotes.text: These comments endorse the above principles while expanding on them to ensure that “electrically proximate” or “adjacent” grid assets—assets that can mitigate the constraints imposed by new loads where those constraints actually occur—are also considered in study prioritization, netted against load withdrawals for study purposes, and included in speed-to-power incentive structures.
quotes.concern: Endorses the ANOPR principles but asks that electrically proximate assets be counted in study prioritization and incentives

quotes.text: the existing ANOPR Principle Three, “load and hybrid facilities should be studied together with generating facilities,” should explicitly include study of incremental resources in the relevant grid area,16 whether or not those resources look like traditional utility-scale power plants.
quotes.concern: Asks that Principle Three's joint study explicitly include incremental distributed resources in the relevant grid area

quotes.text: new capacity supporting large loads need not be physically co-located at the facility, but can be distributed across the geographic area within which the new load will impact system constraints, provided that the system operator has the requisite level of control to integrate the facility into operations and system planning.
quotes.concern: Argues supporting capacity need not be co-located if the operator can control it across the constrained area

quotes.text: ANOPR Principle Seven should be broadened to leverage all available resources.
quotes.concern: Asks that Principle Seven be broadened to include all available resources

quotes.text: capacity—including batteries, generation, and demand response (DR)—should be geographically transferable in the form of capacity credits as defined by the wholesale market, or within the TOs system planning paradigm.
quotes.concern: Proposes that supporting capacity be geographically transferable via wholesale capacity credits

quotes.text: To unlock the capabilities of existing distributed capacity, barriers to participation by aggregated resources in capacity, energy, and ancillary service markets should be rectified. Delays in Order No. 2222 implementation, utility data access issues, and other challenges significantly limit the development of latent flexibility today.
quotes.concern: Asks FERC to remove barriers to aggregated DER participation, citing Order 2222 delays and data access issues

quotes.text: FERC could direct the RTOs/ISOs to consider distributed capacity in connecting large loads, while specifics would likely involve contractual arrangements between a distribution utility, the large load, and the distributed capacity provider.
quotes.concern: Suggests FERC direct RTOs/ISOs to consider distributed capacity while leaving specifics to contracts with distribution utilities

bins.name: Reward distributed flexibility in interconnection
bins.description: The filers urge FERC to direct RTOs/ISOs to expedite studies for large loads that bring curtailable or dispatchable distributed assets, and to broaden Principle Seven so it counts incremental distributed capacity such as load flexibility, storage, and generation. They want all available resources, not only traditional generation, recognized in the reform.

bins.name: Count electrically proximate capacity
bins.description: The filers argue supporting capacity need not be physically co-located but can be distributed across the area where the load affects constraints, so long as the operator can control it. They ask that electrically proximate or adjacent assets count in study prioritization and incentives, and that such capacity be geographically transferable via wholesale capacity credits.

bins.name: Extend co-location beyond the fence line
bins.description: The filers treat co-location as a concept that should extend beyond the facility fence line, so capacity distributed across the relevant geographic area qualifies for the same treatment as physically co-located resources.

bins.name: Broaden joint study to distributed resources
bins.description: The filers endorse holistic study of load and generation but ask that the study scope explicitly include incremental distributed resources in the relevant grid area, and that those resources be netted against load withdrawals and considered in study prioritization.

bins.name: Expedite studies for distributed-capacity loads
bins.description: The filers ask FERC to direct RTOs/ISOs to create mechanisms granting expedited interconnection studies to large loads that provide curtailable or dispatchable distributed assets.

bins.name: Include distributed resources in joint study
bins.description: The filers support the joint study of load and generation under Principle Three and ask that it explicitly extend to incremental distributed resources in the relevant grid area, regardless of whether those resources resemble utility-scale plants.

bins.name: No position on FERC jurisdiction
bins.description: The filers expressly decline to take a position on whether FERC has jurisdiction to issue a rulemaking on large load interconnection.

bins.name: Distributed capacity deploys faster
bins.description: The filers argue distributed capacity can be installed and aggregated into utility-scale assets in months rather than the years new generation requires, making it a bridge resource matched to the urgency of large-load growth.

bins.name: Geographically transferable capacity credits
bins.description: The filers propose that supporting capacity, including batteries, generation, and demand response, be made geographically transferable through wholesale capacity credits or within transmission owner system planning.

bins.name: Remove barriers to aggregated DER
bins.description: The filers ask FERC to rectify barriers to aggregated resource participation in capacity, energy, and ancillary service markets, citing delays in Order No. 2222 implementation and utility data access issues. They note FERC could direct RTOs/ISOs to consider distributed capacity while leaving specifics to contracts with distribution utilities.
